(ステイゴールド, [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") name: 黃金旅程, March 24, 1994 – February 5, 2015) was a Japanese [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") [racehorse](/wiki/Racehorse "Racehorse") who had his greatest success in international races. He was sired by [Sunday Silence](/wiki/Sunday_Silence "Sunday Silence") and was out of the mare Golden Sash, a daughter of [Dictus](/w/index.php?title=Dictus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dictus (page does not exist)"). He was famously known as a "Silver Collector", as he often placed second but was unable to win in major grade 1 races. He was later given the nickname of "Godolphin Killer" after he notably won both the [Dubai Sheema Classic](/wiki/Dubai_Sheema_Classic "Dubai Sheema Classic") and [Hong Kong Vase](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Vase "Hong Kong Vase") towards the end of his career.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In both races, he defeated notable Godolphin horses, Fantastic Light and Ekraar respectively. He also became a successful sire in Japan after his retirement from racing.

**Stay Gold** (ステイゴールド, [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") name: 黃金旅程, March 24, 1994 – February 5, 2015) was a Japanese [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") [racehorse](/wiki/Racehorse "Racehorse") who had his greatest success in international races. He was sired by [Sunday Silence](/wiki/Sunday_Silence "Sunday Silence") and was out of the mare Golden Sash, a daughter of [Dictus](/w/index.php?title=Dictus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dictus (page does not exist)"). He was famously known as a "Silver Collector", as he often placed second but was unable to win in major grade 1 races. He was later given the nickname of "Godolphin Killer" after he notably won both the [Dubai Sheema Classic](/wiki/Dubai_Sheema_Classic "Dubai Sheema Classic") and [Hong Kong Vase](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Vase "Hong Kong Vase") towards the end of his career.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In both races, he defeated notable Godolphin horses, Fantastic Light and Ekraar respectively. He also became a successful sire in Japan after his retirement from racing.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stay_Gold_(horse)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Stay Gold was bred at the Shadai Group's Shiraoi Farm in [Shiraoi, Hokkaido](/wiki/Shiraoi,_Hokkaido "Shiraoi, Hokkaido"). He was notable for being a very small horse, standing 15.3 [hands](/wiki/Hand_(unit) "Hand (unit)") (160 [cm](/wiki/Centimetre "Centimetre")) tall at maturity,[[2]](#cite_note-2) and weighing a maximum of 436 kilograms (961 lb) during his racing career. One of his defining traits other than his rather small physical stature was his personality - Stay Gold was a particularly violent and bad mannered horse, with his trainer Yasutoshi Ikee remarking that "he was so violent, he might even eat meat if I gave it to him,"[[3]](#cite_note-3) and his jockey Shigefumi Kumazawa saying that Stay Gold would rear, kick and bite even during regular exercise.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Racing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stay_Gold_(horse)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Racing career")]

Stay Gold made his racing debut at [Hanshin Racecourse](/wiki/Hanshin_Racecourse "Hanshin Racecourse") on December 1, 1996 and won his first race, a 4 year-old [maiden race](/wiki/Maiden_race "Maiden race") on 11 May 1997. He would then go on to win 2 more races, the latter of which being the "[Lake Akan-ko](/wiki/Lake_Akan "Lake Akan") special (阿寒湖特別)" on September 6, 1997. He would not win another race for approximately two years and eight months, from 6 September 1997, to 20 May 2000.[[5]](#cite_note-nk-5)

In the years of 1998 to 2000, Stay Gold ran prominently in many of Japan's top races, including the Diamond Stakes, [Tenno Sho (Spring)](/wiki/Tenno_Sho "Tenno Sho"), [Takarazuka Kinen](/wiki/Takarazuka_Kinen "Takarazuka Kinen"), [Arima Kinen](/wiki/Arima_Kinen "Arima Kinen"), and Tenno Sho (Autumn). He collected 9 places and 7 shows, but victory proved elusive.

Despite the fact that he had not won any [graded races](/wiki/Graded_stakes_race "Graded stakes race") thus far, Stay Gold accumulated significant earnings due to his consistent podium placements. His title was "Major Racing Wins: Lake Akan-ko special" and he was deemed "the successor to [Nice Nature](/wiki/Nice_Nature "Nice Nature")," another Japanese racehorse who won 6 graded races but was better known for his many placed efforts.

On May 20, 2000, he was ridden by [Yutaka Take](/wiki/Yutaka_Take "Yutaka Take") and in the [Meguro Kinen](/wiki/Meguro_Kinen "Meguro Kinen") and obtained victory for the first time in 2 years and 8 months. It would be his only win of the year.

The year 2001 began with Stay Gold winning first place in the Grade II [Nikkei Shinshun Hai](/wiki/Nikkei_Shinshun_Hai "Nikkei Shinshun Hai"). He then aimed at the [Dubai Sheema Classic](/wiki/Dubai_Sheema_Classic "Dubai Sheema Classic") (UAE-G2:then) at [Nad Al Sheba Racecourse](/wiki/Nad_Al_Sheba_Racecourse "Nad Al Sheba Racecourse"). [Fantastic Light](/wiki/Fantastic_Light "Fantastic Light"), winner of the previous year's [World Series Racing Championship](/w/index.php?title=World_Racing_Championships&action=edit&redlink=1 "World Racing Championships (page does not exist)"), was the clear favourite, with Stay Gold a 33-1 shot. Fantastic Light took the lead a furlong out, but Stay Gold rallied to get up on the line, winning by a nose after finding an opportunity to move through a gap in the final straight.[[6]](#cite_note-6) It was the first victory outside Japan for Sunday Silence's progeny.

In October 2001, he took on two of Japan's best thoroughbreds, [T M Opera O](/wiki/T_M_Opera_O "T M Opera O") and [Narita Top Road](/wiki/Narita_Top_Road "Narita Top Road"), in the [Kyoto Daishoten](/wiki/Kyoto_Daishoten "Kyoto Daishoten") and attained first place. However, during the race he cut off Narita Top Road, injuring both the horse and his jockey, and leading to his [disqualification](/wiki/Ejection_(sports) "Ejection (sports)") from the race.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

His last run was [Hong Kong Vase](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Vase "Hong Kong Vase") at [Sha Tin Racecourse](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Racecourse "Sha Tin Racecourse"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"). There, he wore a saddle towel with his name written as "黃金旅程" in the [Traditional Chinese language](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_language "Traditional Chinese language"), which translates to "Golden Journey". "黃金旅程" is pronounced in [standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") as "huáng jīn lǔ chéng". On the final straight, Stay Gold closed a gap of approximately 5 lengths and caught up to [Ekraar](/wiki/Ekraar "Ekraar") in the final strides and won by a head.[[9]](#cite_note-9) After the race, Yutaka Take, who rode him, said, "Wings grew on his back." At the end of the season, Stay Gold was awarded by the [Japan Racing Association](/wiki/Japan_Racing_Association "Japan Racing Association") with the JRA Special Award for becoming the first overseas G1 race winner that was born and trained in Japan. [[a]](#cite_note-10)

Although initially unplanned, fan outcry and a request from the JRA after his victory at Hong Kong led to Stay Gold's retirement ceremony being performed at [Kyoto Racecourse](/wiki/Kyoto_Racecourse "Kyoto Racecourse") on January 20, 2002.[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12)

## Racing form

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stay_Gold_(horse)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Racing form")]

Stay Gold won seven races out of 50 starts with 20 more podiums finishes. The data available is based on JBIS, netkeiba, racingpost and HKJC.[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[5]](#cite_note-nk-5)[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15)

## Stud record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stay_Gold_(horse)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Stud record")]

At the end of his racing career, Stay Gold was retired to become a breeding stallion and proved to be a very successful sire, fathering 13 Group 1 winners. [Orfevre](/wiki/Orfevre "Orfevre") won the [Japanese Triple Crown](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing#Japan "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing") and finished second in the [Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe](/wiki/Prix_de_l%27Arc_de_Triomphe "Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe") for two consecutive years. [Gold Ship](/wiki/Gold_Ship "Gold Ship") won six Grade 1 races including the [Arima Kinen](/wiki/Arima_Kinen "Arima Kinen"), [Kikuka Shō](/wiki/Kikuka_Sh%C5%8D "Kikuka Shō"), and [Takarazuka Kinen](/wiki/Takarazuka_Kinen "Takarazuka Kinen") twice; [Dream Journey](/wiki/Dream_Journey "Dream Journey") and  [Fenomeno](/wiki/Fenomeno_(horse) "Fenomeno (horse)") also achieved considerable successes.

He died suddenly on February 5, 2015, at the age of 20, due to an arterial rupture.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stay_Gold_(horse)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

An [anthropomorphized](/wiki/Moe_anthropomorphism "Moe anthropomorphism") version of Stay Gold appears in *[Umamusume: Pretty Derby](/wiki/Umamusume:_Pretty_Derby "Umamusume: Pretty Derby")*, voiced by [Satsumi Matsuda](/wiki/Satsumi_Matsuda "Satsumi Matsuda"). She is depicted as a petite woman with dark-brown hair and slightly-torn trench coat who enjoys travelling for long periods of time.[[16]](#cite_note-17) Initially, Stay Gold was represented by "Kin'iro Ryotei", a background character in the first season of the franchise's [anime adaptation](/wiki/Umamusume:_Pretty_Derby_(TV_series) "Umamusume: Pretty Derby (TV series)"), before officially being featured in the Japanese version of the mobile game in 2025.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20)
